coredata.dc:title: DNA methyltransferase activity detection based on graphene quantum dots using fluorescence and fluorescence anisotropy
coredata.dc:creator: Kermani, Hanie Ahmadzade
Hosseini, Morteza
Dadmehr, Mehdi
Hosseinkhani, Saman
Ganjali, Mohammad Reza
coredata.dc:description: DNA methyltransferase enzyme has a crucial role in many biological processes. Abnormal expression level of this enzyme has been regarded as a predictive cancer biomarker. Herein a novel method based on DNA modified GQDs was developed for fluorescence based assay and anisotropic investigation of M.SssI activity. An amine modified ds-DNA was designed including a recognition site for both methyltransferase M.SssI and endonuclease HpaII. By conjugation of ds-DNA to GQDs, fluorescence was decreased to 45%. When M.SssI was introduced, ds-DNA was methylated at palindromic sequence 5′…CCGG…3′ and become resistant to cleavage by HpaII and no change was observed in fluorescence intensity. Contrarily without sufficient M.SssI enzyme, HpaII cleaved DNA strands and induced enhancement of fluorescence. The proposed method exhibited a low detection limit of 0.7U/ml. Additional fluorescence anisotropy method was performed to confirm the DNA modification and release of DNA strands from the surface of GQDs in the presence of M.SssI and HpaII respectively. The application of the assay in the serum sample was successfully investigated. This simple method can be a useful tool to apply in diagnosis and biomedical research.
coredata.dcterms:subject: DNA methyltransferase activity
Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Anisotropy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Principle of GQDs based system for DNA methyltransferase activity detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of M.SssI activity flourimetric assay.
Schematic illustration of the fluorescence anisotropy of M.SssI activity detection.
The fluorescence spectra of GQDs, dsDNA-GQDs, dsDNA-GQDs+40U/ml HpaII, dsDNA-GQDs+15U/ml MTase, dsDNA-GQDs+40U/ml HpaII+15U/mlM.SssI.

A. FAluorescent signal response with different concentrations of M.SssI. M.SssI concentration increased from top to down: 0,2,8,15,20,30U/mL. B. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and concentration of M.SssI.
Selectivity of the proposed assay towards interference enzymes of MssI and DpnI.
Flourescence anisotropy of (1) GQDs, (2) GQDs+dsDNA, (3) GQDs+dsDNA+40U/ml HpaII, (4) GQDs+ dsDNA+ 30U/mlM.SssI.
The linear relationship between the fluorescence anisotropy change and concentration of M.SssI.
A comparison of the proposed method with other fluorescence detection based reported assay.

row.entry: Hairpin fluorescence DNA probe based assay

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recovery of M.SssI methyl transferase added in human serum.

head.ce:title: DNA methyltransferase activity detection based on graphene quantum dots using fluorescence and fluorescence anisotropy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Life Science Engineering, Faculty of New Sciences & Technologies, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology, Payame Noor University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemistry, Tarbiat Modares University, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center of Excellence in Electrochemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Biosensor Research Center, Endocrinology & Metabolism Molecular-Cellular Sciences Institute, Tehran University of Medical Sciences, Tehran, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: University of Tehran, Faculty of New Sciences and Technologies, Amir Ababd Street, Tehran, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: DNA methyltransferase enzyme has a crucial role in many biological processes. Abnormal expression level of this enzyme has been regarded as a predictive cancer biomarker. Herein a novel method based on DNA modified GQDs was developed for fluorescence based assay and anisotropic investigation of M.SssI activity. An amine modified ds-DNA was designed including a recognition site for both methyltransferase M.SssI and endonuclease HpaII. By conjugation of ds-DNA to GQDs, fluorescence was decreased to 45%. When M.SssI was introduced, ds-DNA was methylated at palindromic sequence 5′…CCGG…3′ and become resistant to cleavage by HpaII and no change was observed in fluorescence intensity. Contrarily without sufficient M.SssI enzyme, HpaII cleaved DNA strands and induced enhancement of fluorescence. The proposed method exhibited a low detection limit of 0.7U/ml. Additional fluorescence anisotropy method was performed to confirm the DNA modification and release of DNA strands from the surface of GQDs in the presence of M.SssI and HpaII respectively. The application of the assay in the serum sample was successfully investigated. This simple method can be a useful tool to apply in diagnosis and biomedical research.

ce:section.ce:para: DNA methylation, a prevalent epigenetic modification, has close relationship to gene activation, gene imprinting, chromatin stability, X-chromosome inactivation in females and various diseases such as cancers [1]. It has been confirmed that DNA methylation is catalyzed with DNA methyltransferase by transferring methyl group to DNA using the cofactor S-adenosyl-l-methionine (SAM) [2]. Many studies have indicated that abnormal activity of DNA methyltransferase in CpG islands can result in hypermethylation which can develop cancers by silencing tumor suppressor genes in mammals and virulence in bacteria [3–6]. In particular DNA methyltransferase enzyme (till now abbreviated DNA MTase) have become a significant biomarker of diseases especially cancers [7–10]. Therefore, the detection of DNA methyltransfrase activity could be a critical strategy in life science research. To date, some traditional methods utilize for DNA methylation detection and assay of DNA MTase activity including radioactive labeling of [mrthul-3H]-SAM [11], Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) [12,13], High Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) [14], western blotting [15], bisulfite treatment [16] and immune reaction [17]. Although the above methods have their own advantages but they have some challenges such as time consuming, DNA consuming, using expensive, sophisticated and large scale instruments. Recently new strategies such as electrochemical biosensors [18,19], colorimetric assay [20], fluorescence, chemiluminescence and biochemiluminescence [21–23] and photoelectrochemistry methods [24] have been established. These methods are more advanced but it is necessary to develop more sensitive, rapid, effective and facile strategies for the detection of methylation and DNA MTase activity.

Carbon based nanodots are classified into three categories: (1) Carbon nanodots (CNDs): amorphous quasi-special nanodots which lacks in quantum confinement. (2) Carbon quantum dots (CQDs): spherical nanodots present quantum confinement and have crystalline structure. (3) Graphene quantum dots (GQDs): π-conjugated single sheet or disks of graphene in the size range of 2–20nm [25]. Graphene quantum dots have shown unique electro-optical properties and outstanding performance in photovoltaic devices photocatalysis, biosensing and biological imaging [26]. Compared with conventional dye molecules including organic or inorganic fluorophores they exhibited good photostability, low toxicity, high quantum yield, easy modulation, low costly, resistant to photobleaching and excellent biocompatibility [27]. Due to this excellent fluorescence performance and biocompatibility, GQDs are expected to show promising features in bioimaging and biosensing applications [28–35]. DNA modified graphene quantum dots [28,32,33,35–37] and other nanomaterials with quantum confinement [38,39] are used as fluorescent sensing platforms. In this work highly sensitive fluorescence approach for DNA methylation detection and DNA MTase activity assay based on biocompatible GQDs was established. According to this method, DNA CpG methyltransferase (M.SssI) and HpaII are chosen as DNA MTase and methylation resistant endunoclease model enzyme respectively.

As depicted in Scheme 1 the double stranded DNA (ds DNA) used in this study has CCGG palindromic site that is recognized by both M.SssI and HpaII. The DNA probe is connected to GQDs from the end which contains CCGG sequence. The fluorescence intensity is suppressed due to conjugation of DNA strands to GQDs. With sufficient catalytic activity of M.SssI, these sites are methylated and become resistant to cleavage by HpaII. By increasing the amount of M.SssI, more DNA would be methylated and remain connected to GQDs leading to decrease in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence intensity of ds-GQD has a linear relationship with the M.SssI MTase activity in the range of 2–30U/ml, with the detection limit of 0.7U/ml without any amplification which is comparable with previous reports as seen in Table 1 . Additionally, this method can be performed in human serum with satisfying recovery. We have also used the current probe for the detection of fluorescence anisotropy (FA) to assay M.SssI activity in the same concentration range of enzyme (0–30U/ml). The change in GQDs volume or mass via conjugation of DNA strands will alter their FA [40–42] (Scheme 2 ).

Deionized water was used throughout the experimental processes. CpG methyltransferase (M.SssI), S-adenosyl methionine (SAM), restriction endonuclease HpaII were purchased from Thermoscientific Company. According to the supplier, M.SssI is stored at −20°C in a buffer containing 10mM potassium phosphate(pH 7.0), 400mM KCl, 1mM DTT (Dithiothreiol), 1mM EDTA, 0.2mg/ml BSA (Bovine Serum Albumine) and 50%(v/v) glycerol. HpaII was stored at −20°C in Tango buffer.
The DNA sequence oligonucleotides are as follows:
DNA S1 sequence: 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-TGCCGGTGGGAGGGAGAGAGTAATAA-3′
DNA S2 sequence: 5′-TTATTACTCTCCCTCCCACCTCCGGCA-3′

The double stranded DNA was selected from Adenomatous polyposis coli (APC) tumor suppressor gene sequence. Above DNA sequences were synthesized and purified with PAGE by Shanghai Generay Biotech Co. The oligonucleotides were prepared with TE buffer (containing 1M tris–HCl and 0.5ml EDTA, pH 7.5) and kept in −20°C. Citric acid monohydrate,1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC.HCl) and N-hydroxylsuccinimide sodium salt (NHS) were purchased from Merck. Phosphate buffer solution was prepared by mixing stock solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4 at pH 7.4. All chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Fluorescence measurements were performed using a Perkin Elmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer (Bu Kingham Shire, UK). Transmission Electron Microscope (TEM) image was taken using Zeiss, EM10C, 80KV, Germany. UV–vis absorption was measured using Specord 250 spectrophotometer (analytic jena, Germany). Zeta potential was detected using the zetasizer (Marvalen, Model of nano-zs). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was carried out with a bruker Tensor 27 Spectrometer.

The GQDs were synthesized using the method described by Dong et al. [43] Briefly 2g citric acid was put into a 5ml beaker and heated to 200°C by a heating mantle for 30min, until the citric acid of white solid changed to an orange liquid. Then, a certain amount of 10mg/ml NaOH solution was added into the orange liquid drop by drop, with continuously vigorous stirring, until the pH value of the solution was adjusted to 7 to obtain GQDs solution. The obtained GQDs solution was stored at 4°C in the refrigerator.

The GQDs were first diluted with PBS solution (10mM, PH7.4). After that EDC (60mg) and NHS (60mg) were added to the above solution to activate the surface carboxylic group with stirring for 30min at room temperature. Then amine modified ssDNA (10μl, 100μM) added to resulting solution through condensation reaction for 24h at room temperature with continuous stirring. To remove the free nonconjugated DNA, the ssDNA-GQD were separated by centrifugation at 16300× g for 20min and washed with PBS buffer three times. Afterward, the complementary DNA strands were added into the resulted solution and were incubated for 1h in 37°C to form S1-S2 DNA- GQD. To confirm the immobilization of DNA on GQDs the zeta potential value of the GQDs before and after DNA modification was measured. Zeta potential value changed to −9.5 from −4.6 after modification. This negative shift is due to negative charge of functionalized DNA. In addition, Circular dichroism (CD) spectra of DNA and DNA-GQD indicating a change in the conformation when DNA binds to GQD [36] (Fig. 5S).

In a typical experiment M.SssI methylation was performed by incubating the 50μl dsDNA-GQDs solution and a desire amount of M.SssI, SAM and 1× M.SssI buffer (10mM Tris–HCl, 100mM KCl, 1mM MgCl2, 0.1mg/ml BSA, pH 7.5) at 37°C for 30min. Subsequently the above solution was incubated with 40U/ml HpaII and 1×Tango buffer (33mM Tris-acetate, 10mM Magnesium acetate, 66mM potassium acetate, 0.1mg/ml BSA) per each reaction at 37°C for 20min. The fluorescence spectra were recorded by fluorescence spectrometer.

For the measurement of the fluorescence anisotropy, the FAs of samples were measured using a Perkin Elmer LS-45 fluorescence spectrometer (Bu Kingham Shire, UK). During the assay the similar procedure were performed with mentioned above method. The fluorescence anisotropy of the standard sample was set to 0.206 and blank correction protocol was used for FA of samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of GQDs based system for DNA methyltransferase activity detection
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs possess high biocompability, strong florescence performance and good photo stability which make them the best choice as nano fluorophores in bio detection applications. Scheme 1 shows a schematic illustration of the strategy based on difference of fluorescence intensity between GQDs and dsDNA-GQDs in the presence of different concentration of M.SssI, SAM and 40U/ml of HpaII per each reaction. In this approach GQDs are modified with the double stranded DNA probe that contains a restriction site for HpaII and an amine modifier at 5′ terminus in one of the two strands. Binding the double strands to GQDs leads to ∼45% decrease in fluorescent intensity relative to that of GQDs. By incubating the dsDNA-GQDs with M.SssI these sites are methylated with catalytic activity of M.SssI. The HpaII restriction site is located on the 5′ end which binds to the GQDs, so HpaII could recognize the unmethylated ds sequence of CCGG and cleaves it. In this way it can recover the fluorescence intensity by separation of dsDNA from GQDs surface (Fig. 1 ). Therfore methylation or DNA MTase activity can be detected from fluorescence recovery efficiencies.

It has been discussed elsewhere that surface state of GQDs has significant effect on its fluorescence emission [44–48]. When amine modified DNA strands bond to GQDs with electrostatic interaction, DNA strands with its negative charge can influence the surface passivation layer of GQDs leading to fluorescence quenching. Decrease of zeta potential of GQDs from −4.6 to −9.5mV, confirmed that the surface coating by DNA had changed the surface state of the GQDs. Addition of different amount of M.SssI and 40U/ml HpaII enzyme to system led to separation of unmethylated DNA from GQDs and consequently decrease of the net charge and recovery of fluorescence intensity. Thus, fluorescence recovery is proportional to M.SssI activity which could be used as a detection system.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image in Fig. 1S clearly shows that GQDs are well dispersed and have size distribution which DLS histogram has confirmed it (Fig. 2S). FTIR spectrum was used for characterization of chemical groups on GQDs (Fig. 3S). The UV–vis spectra were carried out to further confirm the formation of GQDs. The UV–vis absorption of GQDs, showed an absorption band centered at 345nm while that citric acid exhibited no obvious absorption band from 300 to 700nm (Fig. 4S-A). The solution of GQDs presented bluish green under UV lamp while it is colorless and transparent under visible light suggested the formation of GQDs (Fig. 4S-B).

The methylation and cleavage reaction were conducted in PBS buffer (pH 7.4) at 37°C. Fig. 2 A shows the trend of fluorescence of proposed system upon addition of different concentration of M.SssI and 40U/ml HpaII. As shown the fluorescence is gradually decreased with increasing of M.SssI concentration within the range of 0–30U/ml. Also it can be seen in Fig. 2B that ΔF/F0 is proportional to M. SssI concentration from 2 to 30U/ml with correlation coefficient 0.99. The linear relationship can be described as ΔF/F0=0.011C+0.077 where C is the concentration of M.SssI and the detection limit is estimated 0.7U/ml for M.SssI which is acceptable in comparison to previously reported fluorescence M.SssI assays [45–48].

The selectivity of the proposed assay was investigated using MssI and DpnI endonucleases. These enzymes are restriction enzymes that have potential to interference with HpaII enzyme. Dpn I specifically recognize methylated 5′ GmATC 3′ and MssI digests the double stranded DNA recognition sequence of 5′ GTTTAAAC3′ which are not exist in our DNA sequence. Fig. 3 shows that 40U/ml of HpaII could induce a significant fluorescence increase. In contrast MssI and DpnI at the same concentration could not induce remarkable fluorescence changes.

To evaluate the assay performance in complex biological samples, we investigate three different concentrations of M.SssI MTase spiked in standard human serum sample. The human serum sample was diluted 1:10 ratio by 10mM phosphate buffer. Each test repeated three times for a certain concentration of M.SssI and the average of detected concentration for every test was calculated. Then, the range of recovery and RSD were reported based on the average detected concentrations. As shown in Table 2 it is observed that recoveries were in the range of 90.2% to 102% with a maximum RSD of 10.8%. These results indicate that the method holds a great promise for the detection of DNA methyltransferase in complex biological matrices.

In summary we developed a fluorescence based strategy to convert mehyltransferase M.SssI activity into florescence signal in the presence of methylation resistant endonuclease enzyme HpaII. Binding of amine modified DNA with negative charge could influence the passive surface layer of GQDs and quench fluorescence. The assay was based on the fluorescence recovery of dsDNA-GQDs after recognition and cleavage of HpaII. Using proposed method 0.7U/mlM.SssI can be detected without further amplification which was acceptable compare to some other previous works. Additionally to confirm the bioconjugation of DNA to GQDs and release of DNA from the surface of GQDs in the presence of methyltransferase M.SssI and endonuclease HpaII, fluorescence anisotropy is successfully investigated. This method was simple, low cost and uses convenient protocol. It can be extended to other DNA methyltransferases because it uses a methylation resistant endonuclease rather than methylation specific endonucleases. The application in human serum sample demonstrates that the assay has good performance in complex biological matrices, so this method can be applied for diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Research Council of University of Tehran for financial support of this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.10.078.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Bisulfite modification of immobilized DNAs for methylation detection
Ultrasensitive electrochemical immunoassay for DNA methyltransferase activity and inhibitor screening based on methyl binding domain protein of MeCP2 and enzymatic signal amplification

DNA-AuNPs based signal amplification for highly sensitive detection of DNA methylation, methyltransferase activity and inhibitor screening, Biosens
Highly sensitive detection of CpG methylation in genomic DNA by AuNP-based colorimetric assay with ligase chain reaction
DNA methyltransferase activity detection based on fluorescent silver nanocluster hairpin-shaped DNA probe with 5'-C-rich/G-rich-3' tails
One-pot synthesis of GO/AgNPs/luminol composites with electrochemiluminescence activity for sensitive detection of DNA methyltransferase activity
A highly sensitive label-free sensor for Mercury ion (Hg2⁺) by inhibiting thioflavin T as DNA G-quadruplexes fluorescent inducer
A novel signal-on strategy for M.SssI methyltransfease activity analysis and inhibitor screening based on photoelectrochemical immunosensor
Semiconductor and carbon based fluorescent nanodots: the need for consistency
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Growing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, synthesis, and biological applications
Graphene quantums dots combined with endonuclease cleavage and bidentate chelation for highly sensitive electrochemiluminescent DNA biosensing
Graphene quantum dots as a new substrate for immobilization and direct electrochemistry of glucose oxidase: application to sensitive glucose determination
Graphene quantum dots-based platform for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors
A universal fluorescence sensing strategy based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide for the detection of DNA
Enhancing cell nucleus accumulation and DNA cleavage activity of anti-cancer drug via graphene quantum dots
Selective recognition of Glutamate based on fluorescence enhancement of graphene quantum dot
DNA nanosensor based on biocompatible graphene quantum dots and carbon nanotubes
Complexation and intercalation modes: a novel interaction of DNA and graphene quantum dots
DNA-modified graphene quantum dots as a sensing platform for detection of Hg2+ in living cells
Carboxylic carbon quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for DNA detection
Fluorescence polarization assays in small molecule screening
A sensitive fluorescence anisotropy method for point mutation detection by using core–shell fluorescent nanoparticles and high-fidelity DNA ligase
A sensitive fluorescence anisotropy method for detection of lead (II) ion by a G-quadruplex-inducible DNA aptamer
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
A facile microwave avenue to electrochemiluminescent two-color graphene quantum dots
Recent advances in graphene quantum dots for sensing
Label-free fluorescence detection of DNA methylation and methyltransferase activity based on restriction endonuclease HpaII and exonuclease III
Versatile DNAzyme-based amplified biosensing platforms for nucleic acid, protein, and enzyme activity detection
Sensitive analysis of DNA methyltransferase based on a hairpin-shaped DNAzyme
Hairpin fluorescence DNA probe for real-time monitoring of DNA methylation
Rapid restriction enzyme free detection of DNA methyltransferase activity based on DNA template silver nanoclusters
A carbon nanoparticle-based low background biosensing platform for sensitive and label free fluorescent assay of DNA methylation

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hanie Ahmadzadeh is a Ph.D. student in nanobiotechnology in University of Tehran.

Morteza Hosseini has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at Tarbiat Modares University in 2005. He is currently an associated professor in nanobiotechnology group of Faculty of New Sciences and Technologies of University of Tehran. His research work has been mainly focused on the design of new optical nanosensors and nanobiosensors.
Mehdi Dadmehr has a Ph.D. in nanobiotechnology obtained at University of Tehran in Jun 2015. He is currently an assistant professor of Biotechnology in University of Payame Nor.

Saman Hosseinkhani has a Ph.D. in biochemistry obtained at University of Tehran in 2000. He is currently a professor of biochemistry at University of Tarbiat Modares. His research work has been mainly focused on the design of new biosensors and mechanism of bioluminescence.

Mohammad Reza Ganjali has a Ph.D. in analytical chemistry obtained at University of Tehran in 1997. He is currently a professor of analytical chemistry at University of Tehran. His research work has been mainly focused on the design of new chemical sensors.
